skill_gap_heatmap.title: Skills supply-demand gap — Nigeria (2025 estimate)
skill_gap_heatmap.source: UNMAPPED analysis: ILO ILOSTAT + Wittgenstein Centre + World Bank WDI
skill_gap_heatmap.note: Gap score = (employer demand signal) minus (estimated supply from education projection). Positive = shortage. Negative = oversupply.

cohort_automation_exposure.title: Estimated automation exposure — Nigeria youth (15-29)
cohort_automation_exposure.source: UNMAPPED analysis: ILO employment shares + Frey-Osborne (LMIC calibrated)
cohort_automation_exposure.caveat: Estimates based on sector employment shares and occupation-level automation scores. Actual exposure depends on task composition within sectors.

exposure.description: Primarily in routine retail, manufacturing, basic admin, and agricultural sorting
exposure.policy_implication: Highest priority for reskilling programmes. Immediate transition support needed.

exposure.description: Agriculture, transport, construction, food services. LMIC infrastructure limits near-term displacement.
exposure.policy_implication: Build durable skills (digital tools, entrepreneurship) now. Monitor automation infrastructure deployment.

exposure.description: Healthcare, education, ICT, skilled trades. Judgment, care, and complex technical skills.
exposure.policy_implication: Invest in growing these pathways. Address credential recognition barriers.

neet_overview.title: Youth not in education, employment or training — Nigeria
neet_overview.note: Significantly higher than Ghana. Reflects large northern states with low female education participation
neet_overview.policy_implication: At 26.8% NEET rate, a significant share of youth is entirely outside formal tracking systems. These are the users UNMAPPED is designed to reach first.

education_trajectory.title: Education trajectory — Nigeria youth (15-29), 2025→2035
education_trajectory.insight: Nigeria has much larger no-education and primary-only population than Ghana. By 2035, meaningful improvement projected but slower than Ghana trajectory. Massive youth cohort means absolute numbers of out-of-school youth remain high even as percentages improve.
education_trajectory.policy_implication: Rising credential levels do not automatically translate to employment. Skills matching infrastructure must scale alongside education expansion.

data_quality_notes.rationale: All signals grounded in published datasets but involve sectoral aggregation and LMIC calibration assumptions. Individual occupation-level data is more reliable than cohort aggregates.
key_gaps: Informal sector wage data: NBS Labour Force Survey 2022 provides partial coverage; urban informal wages poorly tracked
Occupation-level wage data: Not available at ISCO-08 disaggregation for Nigeria
Youth NEET rate: NBS LFS 2022 estimate; significant measurement uncertainty
Regional variation: National figures mask major Lagos/Abuja vs. rural North divergence
